steps.title: Identify the core perceptual principle
steps.content: People perceive sounds they believe are meaningful as quieter than equally loud noise. In this study, both sounds were played at exactly 60.8 dB — only the participant's perception of meaning differed.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘believed that neither the nature sounds nor "Whole Lotta Love" were at a volume of 60.8 decibels.’: Treats the result as a measurement mistake — the passage describes a genuine perceptual phenomenon, not an error in how the volume was measured or recorded. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘adjusted the volume of the nature sounds to be greater than 60.8 decibels.’: Assumes the participant would adjust the volume — the passage gives the volume as fixed at 60.8 dB for both sounds; no adjustment is implied. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘perceived the nature sounds and "Whole Lotta Love" to be comparably meaningful despite perceiving the music to be quieter.’: Claims the participant perceives both sounds as comparably meaningful — this contradicts the experimental setup, in which nature sounds were treated as background noise and the music was treated as meaningful. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘experienced the nature sounds as louder than "Whole Lotta Love" even though they were not.’: The participant treats nature sounds as noise (not meaningful), so they are perceived as louder than "Whole Lotta Love" at the same physical volume. This follows directly from the principle: meaningful sounds seem quieter. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
